summary:
The Trinity test on July 16, 1945 in the New Mexico desert was the world's first detonation of a nuclear weapon, the culmination of the [[event:2765|Manhattan Project]] — the colossal wartime scientific and industrial program that developed the atomic bomb. The plutonium implosion device, nicknamed The Gadget, detonated with a yield of approximately 21 kilotons of TNT equivalent, creating a fireball visible for hundreds of kilometers, a characteristic mushroom cloud rising to 12 kilometers, and fusing desert sand into a glassy green mineral subsequently called trinitite. The test occurred three weeks before the [[event:2661|atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]].

Watching the test, Manhattan Project director J. Robert Oppenheimer famously recalled a line from the Hindu scripture the Bhagavad Gita: now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds — a reflection of the profound moral gravity of what had been achieved. The Trinity test opened the nuclear age, transforming international relations, military strategy, and human civilization's relationship to the prospect of its own destruction. Within a few years, the Soviet Union (1949), Britain (1952), France (1960), and China (1964) would all test nuclear weapons, beginning the arms race that defined the [[event:113|Cold War]] era.

The existence of nuclear weapons created the doctrine of Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD), in which the certainty of devastating retaliation paradoxically served as a deterrent to their use. The nuclear test ban treaties, non-proliferation regime, and eventually arms reduction agreements of the Cold War era were all built on the foundation of what Trinity initiated on that July morning in the desert.
